apgarScore.description: Score a newborn's condition at 1 and 5 minutes using the five APGAR criteria.

meta.title: APGAR Score Calculator — Newborn Assessment at 1 & 5 Minutes
meta.description: Calculate your newborn's APGAR score from skin color, pulse, reflexes, muscle tone, and breathing. Instant 1- and 5-minute results. Free, no sign-up.

apgarScore.title: APGAR Score Calculator
apgarScore.description: Score a newborn's clinical condition using Virginia Apgar's five-criterion system — each rated 0–2 points, evaluated at 1 and 5 minutes after birth.
apgarScore.advice_reassuring: A 5-minute score of ≥ 7 indicates good neonatal adaptation. Routine post-delivery care (bonding, newborn examination) is sufficient.
apgarScore.advice_moderate: A score of 4–6 typically requires supportive measures: airway clearing, oxygen, or stimulation. Continued close observation by midwife or paediatrician — alert the resuscitation team if values do not improve.
apgarScore.advice_critical: A score of ≤ 3 reflects a life-threatening condition. Immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation and neonatal intensive care are required.
apgarScore.extendedNote: 5-minute score below 7: per guidelines, repeat the assessment every 5 minutes until ≥ 7 or until the newborn is handed over to neonatology.
apgarScore.howItWorksText: The APGAR score was developed in 1953 by anaesthesiologist Virginia Apgar. It rates five vital signs — Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, and Respiration — with 0 to 2 points each. The total (0–10) is conventionally measured at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. Scores ≥ 7 are reassuring, 4–6 indicate moderate depression, and ≤ 3 are critical and require resuscitation.
apgarScore.disclaimer: This calculator helps structure the APGAR assessment and does not replace expert clinical judgement. Newborn evaluation requires hands-on experience. If any vital sign is impaired, immediately involve a midwife, obstetrician, or neonatologist.

faq.a: The APGAR score is a quick newborn-assessment tool created in 1953 by Virginia Apgar. It rates five clinical signs (skin color, pulse, reflexes, muscle tone, breathing) with 0 to 2 points each, summing to a total between 0 and 10.

faq.q: When is the APGAR score taken?
faq.a: Routinely at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. If the 5-minute score is below 7, the assessment is repeated every 5 minutes until it reaches 7 or higher — typically also at 10, 15, and 20 minutes.
Scores between 7 and 10 at 5 minutes are considered reassuring. 4–6 indicates moderate adaptation difficulty, while ≤ 3 reflects a severely depressed newborn requiring immediate resuscitation.

faq.q: Does the APGAR score predict long-term outcome?
faq.a: The 1-minute score only describes immediate adaptation status and is not a prognostic tool. Persistently low 5-minute scores (≤ 3 over 10–20 minutes) correlate with elevated neurologic risk, but most low APGAR babies develop normally.
Usually the midwife or obstetrician, often together with a neonatologist or paediatrician. Standardized, independent evaluation of each sign is essential.

APGAR is both the inventor's name (Virginia Apgar) and an acronym: Appearance (skin color), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), and Respiration (breathing effort).
